title: The Body's Memory of Being Silenced in Childhood
dek: *Your body remembers every time you were told your voice didn't matter. That memory lives in your throat, your chest, your breath. It shapes how you show up in rooms, relationships, and moments that ask you to be seen. This is about learning to speak again, not just with words, but with your whole self.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. When children are repeatedly told to be quiet, their nervous systems encode silence as safety and expression as danger. This somatic imprint persists into adulthood as throat tightness, shallow breathing, and an inability to speak up even when safe. Healing requires re-patterning the body's stress response through gentle vocal practices and reclaiming the right to take up space.

Have you ever opened your mouth to say something that mattered and felt your throat close up like a fist? That sensation isn't just nerves. It's your body remembering every time speaking up wasn't safe. Every time you were shushed at the dinner table. Every time a teacher told you to stop asking so many questions. Every time a parent's exhausted face made it clear that your needs were too much, your feelings too loud, your very presence an inconvenience. The body doesn't forget these moments. It catalogs them in the fascia, the diaphragm, the muscles that control your vocal cords. Years later, you might be in a meeting with colleagues who genuinely want to hear your input, and still your voice comes out small. Or it doesn't come out at all. ## The Physiology of Learned Silence The research backs this up. See [the NIH overview of anger and the autonomic nervous system](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2864403/) for the underlying physiology - the body's response to anger is well documented, even when the cultural script around it is not. When a child is repeatedly silenced, the nervous system learns to associate self-expression with threat. The vagus nerve, which runs from your brainstem down through your throat and into your viscera, becomes conditioned to trigger a shutdown response whenever you prepare to speak your truth. This isn't metaphorical. It's measurable. Gabor Maté's work on childhood emotional suppression demonstrates how children who cannot safely express anger, sadness, or even excitement learn to compress those feelings in the body. The compression has to go somewhere. It settles as chronic tension in the jaw, tightness across the chest, a voice that never quite reaches full volume. You might notice this when you're about to disagree with someone. Your breath gets shallow. Your heart rate spikes. Your hands go cold. These are the same physiological markers that appear during acute stress, because for your nervous system, speaking up *is* acute stress. The body is trying to protect you from a danger it learned about decades ago. In our experience, people often describe this as "losing their voice" in certain situations, but that's not quite accurate. The voice is there. The body is actively preventing it from emerging because silence once equaled survival. ## The Throat as Archive Your throat holds more memory than you realize. The hyoid bone, a small horseshoe-shaped bone that sits just above your Adam's apple, is the only bone in your body not connected to any other bone. It floats, suspended by muscles and ligaments, and it's intimately involved in swallowing, breathing, and speaking. When you were little and you swallowed your words (literally pulled them back down instead of letting them out), those muscles learned a pattern. Tighten, constrict, hold. Over thousands of repetitions, that pattern becomes your default. You might not even notice it anymore. It just feels like who you are: someone who doesn't really have strong opinions, someone who's "easy-going," someone who'd rather keep the peace. But if you put your fingers gently on either side of your throat and try to say something you've been afraid to say, you'll feel those muscles contract. The body is doing exactly what it was trained to do. It's following orders you gave it when you were six years old and learned that the cost of speaking was too high. Thich Nhat Hanh taught that the body and mind are not separate, that every mental formation has a physical counterpart. When we carry the mental formation of "my voice doesn't matter," the body manifests it as literal voicelessness. The teaching isn't to think differently, but to breathe differently, to inhabit the body differently, until the old pattern loosens. ## When Anger Has Nowhere to Go Soraya Chemaly's research on how girls are socialized to suppress rage reveals something crucial: anger that cannot be expressed doesn't disappear. It turns inward. It becomes depression, anxiety, autoimmune conditions, chronic pain. The body becomes the battleground for all the fights you weren't allowed to have. Children, especially girls, are often punished not just for speaking but for the emotional content of their speech. Anger is "unladylike." Sadness is "attention-seeking." Excitement is "too much." So you learn to compress it all into a tight, acceptable package. You learn to smile when you're furious. You learn to say "I'm fine" when you're falling apart. That compression creates enormous internal pressure. You might experience it as tension headaches that start at the base of your skull. Or as a feeling of something stuck in your chest that you can't cough up. Or as the sense that if you really let yourself feel what you feel, you'd shatter into a thousand pieces. Over the years I've seen this show up as an inability to cry even when grief is overwhelming, or as crying at the smallest things because the dam is so full that any crack releases a flood. The body is trying to manage an impossible task: hold everything in and also somehow process it. Some people find that supplementing with [Solgar GABA 500 mg (100 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0001OP014?tag=spankyspinola-20) helps calm the nervous system enough to begin this work, though it's no substitute for actually learning to discharge the stored emotion. ## The Breath You're Not Taking Notice your breathing right now. Is it high and shallow in your chest, or deep and full in your belly? If you're like most people who were silenced in childhood, you're breathing just enough to stay alive, but not enough to feel fully alive. Shallow breathing is a freeze response. It's what happens when the nervous system decides that the best strategy is to be as small and unnoticeable as possible. Children who are repeatedly told to be quiet often become adults who literally take up less space, who make themselves compact, who breathe quietly so as not to disturb anyone. Full diaphragmatic breathing, by contrast, is an assertion. It says: I am here. I am taking up space. I am entitled to the air in this room. For someone whose body learned that taking up space was dangerous, breathing fully can feel almost aggressive at first. Try this: Put one hand on your chest and one on your belly. Breathe normally for a few breaths and notice which hand moves more. If it's the chest hand, your breath is stuck in the upper registers. Now try to breathe so that only the belly hand moves. Let the inhale push your hand forward. Let the exhale soften everything. You might feel emotion rise up when you do this. That's the stored material starting to move. The breath is the gateway. When you breathe differently, you feel differently. When you feel differently, you can speak differently. ## Reclaiming Resonance Your voice has a natural resonance, a frequency that's uniquely yours. When you speak from a place of authentic expression, you can feel it vibrate in your chest, your throat, your skull. It's a full-body experience. But when you're speaking from the silenced place, the voice is thin. It doesn't carry. It sounds apologetic even when you're not apologizing. You might notice yourself ending statements with an upward inflection, turning declarations into questions, as if constantly seeking permission to exist. Vocal tone is regulated by the same vagal pathways that manage your sense of safety in the world. When you feel safe, your voice has more range, more color, more presence. When you don't feel safe, the voice flattens out. It becomes a monotone designed not to provoke. Across the articles we've published on this site, we return again and again to the idea that healing isn't just psychological. It's somatic. You can understand intellectually that you're allowed to speak up now, but if your body still believes that speaking is dangerous, understanding won't change much. You have to re-pattern the body. You have to give it new data. You have to practice speaking in contexts where it's genuinely safe, and let the nervous system recalibrate. This is slow work. The body learns through repetition, not revelation. ## A Five-Minute Practice for Finding Your Voice Here's something you can do right now, wherever you are: Find a private space where you won't be interrupted. Stand up if you're able. Plant your feet hip-width apart. Take three full breaths, letting the exhale be longer than the inhale. Now make a sound. Any sound. A hum, a sigh, a vowel. Don't try to make it pretty or controlled. Just let sound come out of your mouth. Notice where you feel the vibration. Is it in your throat? Your chest? Your head? Now try different pitches. Go lower than feels comfortable. Go higher. Notice which sounds make your body tense up and which ones feel easier. There's information in that difference. Finally, say out loud something you've been afraid to say. It doesn't have to be to anyone. Just speak it into the room. "I'm angry that you didn't listen." "I needed you and you weren't there." "I matter." Notice what happens in your body when you give voice to the silenced thing. You might feel shaky. You might feel a little ridiculous. You might feel a surprising rush of grief or relief. All of that is the body starting to remember that it's allowed to make noise, to take up sonic space, to exist at full volume. ## The Social Architecture of Silence We don't silence ourselves in a vacuum. We do it because we've learned to read rooms, to assess who has power and who doesn't, to modulate our expression based on complex social calculations that happen faster than thought. Women learn this earlier and more thoroughly than men. People of color learn it in contexts where white people don't have to think about it. Queer kids learn it in families and schools that punish deviation. The specific contours vary, but the mechanism is the same: you learn that certain versions of yourself are acceptable and certain versions will get you hurt. The heartbreak is that you internalize the silencing. You don't need anyone to tell you to be quiet anymore. You do it automatically. You edit yourself before the words even form. You have entire conversations in your head that never make it to your mouth. This is adaptive in genuinely dangerous situations. The problem is that the body can't always distinguish between contexts. It applies the same silencing mechanism in your therapist's office that it learned in your childhood kitchen. It makes you small at the dinner table with friends who love you because twenty years ago being small at a different dinner table kept you safe. Part of healing is developing discernment. Learning to ask: Is this situation actually dangerous, or does it just feel dangerous because it's familiar? Do I need to protect myself right now, or is my body running an outdated program? The NIMH page on emotional regulation offers useful frameworks for this kind of assessment, though it doesn't always account for how deeply somatic these patterns are. You can't think your way out of a body memory. You have to move it, breathe it, sound it out. ## Witness and Permission One of the most powerful experiences in healing from childhood silencing is having someone simply witness you speaking your truth without trying to fix it, debate it, or make it more palatable. This is different from venting. Venting is a discharge, often circular, that doesn't necessarily shift anything. Witnessed speaking is when you say the thing that's true and someone receives it without flinching. Their nervous system stays regulated while yours does something it wasn't allowed to do before. That experience of being heard without consequence rewires the threat response. It gives your body new data: I spoke my truth and I'm still safe. The relationship didn't end. The person didn't leave. I didn't get punished. You need this repeated enough times that it becomes more familiar than the old pattern. One conversation won't do it. You need a steady diet of being received as you are, voice and all. This is part of what good therapy provides. It's also what genuine friendship offers, and what the practices at [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com) are designed to support. Some people create little rituals around this. Lighting a candle and speaking aloud to it. Recording voice memos that no one else will hear. Writing letters that won't be sent but that let the words form and be witnessed, even if only by your future self reading them back. The [FES Yarrow Environmental Solution Spray (2-pack)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00F3SKA7M?tag=spankyspinola-20) can be helpful for sensitive people who feel overwhelmed when they start to open up their voice and find themselves more porous to others' reactions. ## The Difference Between Voice and Noise There's a fear that if you stop silencing yourself, you'll become one of those people who talks all the time about nothing. That you'll be loud and obnoxious and take up too much space. This fear is a remnant of the original silencing. It's the internalized voice of whoever told you to be quiet, now disguised as your own discernment. The truth is, people who were silenced in childhood rarely have a problem with talking too much once they heal. They have exquisite sensitivity to others. They know what it feels like to be unheard. They don't want to inflict that on anyone else. What changes is that they stop swallowing the things that need to be said. They stop pretending to agree when they don't. They stop saying yes when they mean no. They stop laughing at jokes that aren't funny. They stop making themselves smaller so others can feel bigger. This isn't the same as being inconsiderate. It's the opposite. It's having enough respect for the people around you to show up as your actual self, which gives them permission to do the same. Real voice has texture. It has silence in it too, but it's chosen silence, not imposed silence. It's the pause that comes from listening, not from fear. ## When the Body Starts to Thaw As you do this work, you might notice physical sensations you've been numb to for years. Your throat might feel raw, like you've been crying even when you haven't. Your chest might feel tender. You might yawn more, which is the nervous system's way of completing a stress cycle that got stuck. You might also notice that you're suddenly exhausted. Speaking up takes energy when you've spent decades compressing. The muscles that hold the silence in place have been working constantly. When they start to release, you feel the fatigue they've been masking. Let yourself rest. This isn't laziness. It's integration. The body needs time to reorganize around a new way of being. A cup of [Yogi Soothing Caramel Bedtime Tea (96 ct)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00511MLLO?tag=spankyspinola-20) before bed can support this integration phase, helping the nervous system settle as it rewires. You might also find yourself saying things you didn't plan to say. The words just come out. This can be alarming, but it's often the body catching up, releasing what's been held too long. As long as you're not harming anyone, let it happen. You can always clarify later. The important thing is that the channel is opening. ## What Your Silence Protected Here's something that doesn't get talked about enough: your silence might have been protecting someone else's comfort, but it was also protecting you from having to face how angry you really are. If you let yourself feel the full weight of how many times you were dismissed, ignored, talked over, told your feelings didn't matter, told you were too sensitive, told you were making things up - if you really let yourself feel that - it's volcanic. And maybe some part of you knows that if you start speaking, that volcano is going to erupt. So you keep it locked down. You tell yourself it wasn't that bad. You make excuses for the people who silenced you. You focus on understanding their perspective. This is spiritual bypassing wearing a psychological mask. Real healing requires you to stop protecting the people who hurt you and start protecting the child you were. That child needed someone to say: you have every right to be furious. What happened to you was not okay. Your anger is information, not a character flaw. You can look at resources like [the fire toolkit](/fire-toolkit) when you're ready to work with anger in a structured way, or explore [the assessments hub](/assessments) to get a clearer picture of how these patterns are showing up in your life right now. But first you have to stop doing the silencer's job for them. You have to stop being the one who shushes yourself. ## Frequently Asked Questions ### What if I start speaking up and people don't like it? Some people won't. The ones who benefited from your silence will be uncomfortable when you find your voice. They might tell you you've changed, that you're not yourself anymore. They're right. You're not the self that made itself small for their convenience. This is a sorting mechanism. The people who stay are the ones who want the real you. The ones who leave were only ever interested in the edited version. Both outcomes give you useful information. ### How do I know if I'm being authentic or just reactive? Reactive speech comes from an activated nervous system. It's hot, fast, and often regretted later. Authentic speech comes from a grounded place. You can feel the difference in your body. Reactive feels like pressure that has to come out right now. Authentic feels like truth that can wait for the right moment but won't be swallowed anymore. Take three breaths before you speak. If the words still need to be said, they're probably authentic. If they dissolve, they were likely reactive discharge. ### Can I heal this without talking about my childhood? You can make progress without excavating every memory, but you can't heal a body pattern without acknowledging where it came from. The body doesn't care about your narrative. It cares about safety. If you can create enough experiences of safe self-expression in the present, the body will gradually update its threat assessment. Some people do this through movement, art, or sound rather than traditional talk therapy. The key is finding a modality that lets the stored material move without retraumatizing you in the process. You can explore [more articles in this journal](/articles) for different approaches to somatic healing. --- The work of reclaiming your voice isn't about becoming someone new. It's about becoming more fully yourself. The self that existed before you learned that silence was safer than sound. That self is still here, waiting underneath all the years of compression. You'll know you're making progress not because you suddenly have all the right words, but because the wrong words stop mattering so much. Because you can stumble through saying something imperfectly and not collapse in shame. Because your throat stays open even when your heart is racing. Because you can take up space in a room without apologizing for the air you breathe. This is the body remembering what it knew before it learned to forget: that your voice, in all its imperfect human realness, has always been worth hearing. ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 29, 2026. In our work on the body's memory of being silenced in childhood, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
